question: Can you have a right and left bundle branch block?
title: Bundle branch block
passage:
Dear Daniel Thompson,

I hope this message finds you well. I’ve been studying bundle branch blocks and came across some fascinating information regarding their diagnosis and implications. A bundle branch block can be diagnosed when the duration of the QRS complex on the ECG exceeds 120 ms. A right bundle branch block typically causes prolongation of the last part of the QRS complex and may shift the heart's electrical axis slightly to the right. The ECG will show a terminal R wave in lead V1 and a slurred S wave in lead I. Conversely, left bundle branch block widens the entire QRS and usually shifts the heart's electrical axis to the left. The ECG will display a QS or rS complex in lead V1 and a monophasic R wave in lead I. An interesting aspect of bundle branch block is appropriate T wave discordance, where the T wave deflects opposite the terminal deflection of the QRS complex. Notably, left bundle branch block can lead to cardiac dyssynchrony, and simultaneous occurrence of both left and right bundle branch blocks can result in total AV block. This leads me to ask: can one really have both right and left bundle branch block simultaneously?
